speaker: interviewee
text: Did you understand--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the poem?

speaker: interviewer
text: Very beautiful. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: There's another one if you want to read it. That was my daughter's way to put it in, because the-- the writing was fading, and I folded it. So I have it under the glass now.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's very beautiful. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And beautiful use of the language, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he had no time later on to write more poems. He was busy with anything else. Working, worrying. He was a big worrier.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. Try again.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape four with Betty Daniel. Betty, you were telling me about life in the United States when you were living in Los Angeles. You were talking about your husband's business in the garment industry. Where was that business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was in Los Angeles, and I have very good recollections from it, although there are advantages and disadvantages to work with a husband. Until today, I am in touch with a few remain workers that are still around. And I was very good adjusting the children to work. Occasionally, they came to help, too. Although I don't know whether that time, it was-- no, it wasn't the right time for my daughter to get into the business. She went to UCLA and then Berkeley. And some knowledge she had from the shop, but it was other roads that she choosed. My son, too. But it was very good recollection from the experience with the garment industry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Betty, going back a little bit. When you first arrived in Los Angeles, what were your expectations of the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: My expectations of the United States-- of course, I had no experience the lifestyle here as I had in Cuba. I-- living conditions were here a little different than in Cuba. Cuba, I was helping my husband in the business, too. But then with the children, they were small. And now there was a little worries, too, with the upbringing with the children, and temple, our social life, neighbors, where to live. It was expectations. I don't know. I wasn't a worrier. I think my husband was the worrier. I was just the moral support. So I cannot say that I worried.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you join a temple? Did you join a temple?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we joined a temple. Yes, we joined a temple and we got the children involved, and my daughter very much liked. Very enthusiastic, very well liked to read, going to Sunday school. And my son, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were temple members right where they-- she was bat mitzvah. My son was bar mitzvah at the same temple. It was in North Hollywood, yeah. Did you find that people wanted to talk about your stories about the war when you came to the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I-- when we came here, up to, I would say the '70s, when my husband passed away, they wasn't as open, the Holocaust, as it is now. And even myself, occasionally we talked with my husband about this. Because I wanted to-- to take-- I wanted to go to Warsaw. One day I-- I said to my husband that there was a wedding to our wealth friend from Paris, and I said that I would like to go. So he said, you go, but-- yourself. So I went. At the same time, I wanted to go--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that, Betty?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1971. So that time, I said to my husband that I would like to go to Warsaw to put up a marker, because his father passed away when the ghetto uprise was. So I-- so he says, nothing of it I want to do with. So he didn't want me to, and I didn't want to do anything behind his back. But after-- when he passed away in '74, it was on my mind to go to Warsaw to put up a marker. And I told my children, I'd like to go to Europe, to Auschwitz and to Warsaw. So the children, being in school and working, never was time. But I was ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: But one day, my dear daughter said, Mom, you want to go? That was in '85-- August, '88. Mom, lets go. So I took a trip. She arranged all the papers and I went with my daughter. And I'm grateful forever to her, that because of her, I went.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Auschwitz, and to my surprise, she takes out a prayer book to say Kaddish on a cemetery on Warsaw over there. I didn't think of it, but she thought to take it. And it was-- I cannot describe the pride what I had. And until today, I am grateful I was there. It was on my mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like for you to return to Poland, to Auschwitz, which was near your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was, for me, to return to Auschwitz, it was sort of like an obligation to shed some tears and to be grateful that I lived to a day that I can do this. And to bring up what my father said. Talk, if you survive, for what happened. The injustice. That was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was there with-- unexpected, I-- I met one of my landsleit, that he was a survivor from Auschwitz. Describe what landsleit is for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: A landsleit is one that was born in the same town that I was born. And he lived in Israel, and he was in Cracow. So visiting there, so I had a chance to contact him. And I told him, look, I am here. Let's go to Auschwitz. He says that he has been so many times in Poland, but he couldn't go to Auschwitz. He couldn't put himself to go. So I said, I need you and you need me. Let's go together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went. And he went into his barrack where he was in Auschwitz. He told me, stay outside until I call you in. So he looked around in his barrack in Auschwitz, and then he called me. And he says, you see, in this corner, I was there. Laying around. And if I am here now, I would-- I don't know what to say. So he was pleased. So, being in Auschwitz, I was with my friend. And I told my daughter, who was the chauffeur, I said, you go and let's meet at a certain time here and here. Because I didn't want to be together. Maybe she wants to see and stay a little longer on one spot, or maybe not.

speaker: interviewee
text: The crematoriums, I did not go into Birkenau. I couldn't. But I was there. I made some pictures, I have some memories, and I'm pleased. And I-- although I had some criticisms from survivors, why I go to Poland. Because I said yes, every one of us said that my foot won't step here when we are gonna survive after the war. But it's sort of like, it awakes in you, like to go, like, to a cemetery. You go to shed some tears. And I feel-- I'm glad to see that a lot, since then, are gone and still going and should. Even recently, I met, a couple weeks ago, someone that couldn't even talk about the camp. He was started to cry. But now, he's going to-- he's now in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, you know what? Don't forget to take a prayer book with you. Have the prayer book with you. This prayer book will-- will give you a little-- a little [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] future, a little hope to be content. To for-- not to forget, but to live with that what you went through, and to be thankful that you lived to a time that you can do it. Betty, so you-- your Judaism, your faith, your belief--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Strong. Remained strong--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --throughout your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel that I survived-- you see, there is a saying. Jews and bed bugs, you cannot finish. You know what a bed bug is? You cannot finish. There's always remain one. And this one should spread more and more and more Jews around. We cannot-- You cannot destroy it. We are tough. As much as we are hated, we are tough. With so many persecutions, we have to keep our-- our face up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Betty, your daughter, Anna, who went with you to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you discussed your experiences with her while she was growing up? With her and your son?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is a very touchy subject, because she complains that her father didn't talk much. We were closed. And I didn't want to put any hatred. But now, a little more than before. Not as much maybe that she would like, or maybe I would like. But it's a-- and I am willing. I am willing. But my son is a little different. Many times, to watch the television, I like to see some programs, but then I turn off. I cannot take it. But I think my son will-- because he has a family. And sooner or later the children will grow up, and he will face certain questions.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I put some, in writing, to say my history and my experiences, although I didn't have horror as much those who were in Auschwitz. My recollections are the [INAUDIBLE] and the goodies from-- from the Red Cross. Not too many had, and maybe some of them had them and they didn't survive. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were some. How did being a survivor affect how you raised your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: How I raised my children?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I tried, and I see now, I-- that, even when I visit with my son, there is no waste of food. If I see my children-- my grandchildren don't like certain things that my son-- he ate the-- the whole slice of bread. He didn't eat the crust. He ate the crust. But his son doesn't eat the crust, so my son eats the crust.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's wrong with the crust? The crust is very good. So I am proud that I don't see any waste, and I didn't impose on them. Betty, why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why I think I survived? The outlook. Pure luck.

speaker: interviewer
text: 50 years after the war, here we are. Do you have a message for your grandchildren and for the future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, I think that I will be an example with communicating. To communicate is very important. Second, not to hate. I can forgive for myself, but I cannot forgive for what happened to my parents. Innocent. To finish, to spent-- not to have the life. A little-- a little joy, which they had very little. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Try to forgive as much as you can, with compromise. Give. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Forget what you give, but remember what you receive. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My way of living, I hope and pray that I live to a time-- I just turned 74-- that I will be able to-- to have my grandson's bar mitzvah, God willing. Take care of myself. I am proud of Betty Daniel. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Although sometimes, listen. Talk less and do more, or practice what you preach. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I live in Santa Monica, close to the beach. I enjoy it. Count your blessings. Sometimes I wonder if I know enough arithmetic, whether I count enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: Another things that is in my-- and I can't help it-- superstition. To talk less, evil eye. A drop remains. You cannot help it. As much as I don't like to, some people say it's [YIDDISH]. You know what [YIDDISH] is? Fairy tale. I let it go. But occasionally, I stick to them. But goodness and kindness-- What are some of your superstitions?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember when my daughter was little, I-- she was such a little darling. She still is. I asked my sister to send me some red coral to put her some earrings. Because red coral was in our-- well, say-- I wouldn't say tradition-- custom, to put on for evil eye. Because she was so pretty, somebody might give her a evil eye.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what-- [SIGHS] what else superstition? I can't think of anything, but certain things still remain. Sometimes I hide them, invisible. I try not to show them. When I get-- sharing. I get a box of chocolate. I try to-- to cut the pieces in half. Why do you cut them in half? Can't you afford the whole piece? I said, well, it's too much, and every one is a different kind, so I want to do it. So I want to have a taste from every one of them. I take a knife and I cut them in half. Or if I see some goodie, some pastry. How can you eat a whole piece? Who wants to be my partner? Of course I get partners. And then I get followers. So certain things-- well, this I wouldn't call superstition, but sometimes, the sharing is my life. Goodness and kindness, never too much. Although I got my disappointments, too, but I try to cope with it as much as I can. I put it on a paper.

speaker: interviewee
text: It looks like-- my daughter is a writer. Maybe she gets the writing from me, or maybe I got it now from her. She wrote a beautiful book, visiting Bali, Indonesia. Wrote it, accomplished. Beautiful photographer. She has a-- she is gifted, which we never thought that, being a survivor, we're gonna have gifted children. Intelligent, knowledgeable. Reading and writing. Open-minded, giving, compassionate. Which I hope they will grow into a world, a peaceful, and they will not experience what I experience. And my lifestyle will-- will lead them to peace.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Betty. That was very beautiful. Thanks for sharing your testimony.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to mention pictures?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Now we're gonna look at your pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my blouse that I got in 1945. The first gift, a new blouse, which I got it in Ochtrup, in Germany. A gift from the mayor of the town. We were clothed from top to bottom, and this is the blouse I retain with one hole, which was-- well, it's-- it's a holy blouse, I call it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Show us.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't wear it, I don't fit in it anymore--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you show us the hole?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you show us the front, the hole?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the hole is right here. I can stick in my finger. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you select that blouse, Betty?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that hole was eaten in Cuba. When I lived in Cuba, a cockroach, I think, ate it up. Nothing else. That was my recollection.

speaker: interviewer
text: Betty, did you choose that blouse when you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I chose that blouse, yes. And now I like it, and until today I-- I like polka dots. So I retained this blouse. But I got another coat, which I gave away the other one, but I got another one. So this is the blouse. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is my picture. I got document in Landsberg DP camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who-- name yourself, the name of the person in the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of the person is Helfer, Betty, my maiden name. Jewish-- Jewish Former Political Prisoner Committee, Landsberg, Bavaria.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in-- I think on the bottom it says in 1946. 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was made in Landsberg. It was given to me just before I-- I went for Paris. Prisoner and the number, which I have the DP camp, the ID.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are the people in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this picture is Jacob Daniel and Betty Daniel. Betty Helfer, rather, maiden name. Married in November 26, 1946, in Paris, France. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Without any relatives, just-- just an aunt and uncles and friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this, Betty?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the first card I got in Bergen-Belsen. Allied Exped-- Expeditionary Force DP Index Card, Registration, Helfer, Betty. Family name.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is a birthday card given in Marienheim-- that's what it called, the place where we were recovering from-- as a convalescent. It says, for your birthday, I wish you a new pair of-- no. This is not. This is mine. It is just a plain, I wish you the best everything. As a friend. That was mine. I was just a friend that time. But there is another-- Yeah--

speaker: interviewer
text: To your husband, this was written.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the same-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 15-10-45. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But there is another one. That is it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Describe.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, tell us what--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this birthday card is from my cousin to my husband. In day of your birthday, I wish you, from the bottom of my heart, a new pair of shoes. Dora. Ochtrup, 25th of October, 1945. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] You don't want me to read that?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but just say--

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- no.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you can just say this is the poem that your husband wrote to you right after-- when you met after the war. OK, you can describe this, what it is. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Betty.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the poem that my husband tried to introduce himself to get acquainted and express himself, how much he would like-- and his feelings. Being alone and being sad, and why I am sad. And he would love to see me [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] free, with lots of love, and thinking of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this written?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was written in Ochtrup, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Ochtrup, in the convalescent home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Betty, who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this picture is made in Cuba, just before we left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who are the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: The people is Betty Daniel and Simon Daniel, parents of Simon and Anita Daniel. This is Simon Daniel. Baby, just one year old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is Anita Anna Daniel. You-- you notice she got some earrings over there? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She wears a beautiful velvet dress. Little doll. Little prize-- priceless doll. That was happiness to us, this family picture. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Three and a half years old. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the middle is the girl that took care of them when I was helping my husband in the store.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in Cuba, in 1952 or '53.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the people in the picture?

speaker: interviewer
text: And the people in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: The people in the picture is Anna Daniel, Simon-- Simon Daniel, and-- and the little one who took care of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where-- where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Calimete, Provincia Matanzas, in Cuba.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister, Helen, and Jack--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's--

speaker: interviewee
text: --Zisner, Z-I-S-N-E-R. This is my sole family here now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is done, maybe, I would say, five or six years ago. Well, maybe more than that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's right here in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about a year-- a year for this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture?

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's made in, maybe, I would say in the-- in the late 80s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this? Who are these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is the family picture. Simon Daniel, Anna Daniel, Betty Daniel, and father Simon Daniel-- Jacob Daniel.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the junior high graduation of Simon Daniel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when? What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This must have been-- just a minute. '80-- or maybe late '70-- no. Late '60s.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in North Hollywood. Walter Reed Junior High. Right the earrings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a baby. This is my daughter, Anita.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say-- in Calimete, in Cuba.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born in September. I would say in '49, early '49. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are the people in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this picture is myself, Betty Daniel, and my dear daughter, Anna Daniel. My 60th birthday. It was-- born in '21, and 60th-- 1970-- 1980. No, '81.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in North Hollywood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well in this-- OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: In this picture is my son, Simon Daniel, and his wife, Denise, married in '7-- '87. '86? '87.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Married in-- in Simi Valley, in Calabasas.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, who is in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there are three grandchildren. The middle one is the oldest, is Jacob, named after my husband. The next one is Zachary, and the little girl-- princess, I would say-- Rachel.

speaker: interviewer
text: When and where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: They all are born in Simi Valley, in Moorpark, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken? When?

speaker: interviewee
text: When? The-- the big one is eight-- they're going to be eight, seven and three.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is taken recently?

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken recently, yes. Taken recently.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: The side pictures are my parents, my mother and father, Anna Miller and David Helfer. And the middle, the top, is Simon Daniel and Anna Daniel.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were these pictures taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, those pictures were taken just before-- before the war. This one, my mother was maybe-- maybe it was been made, I would say in the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] maybe in the '4-- [INAUDIBLE] 1939. 19-- 19-- 1930, maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1930. They-- I would say all of them would be, before-- just before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were these taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And about--

speaker: interviewee
text: All those pictures.
